title: Mud on Wool Carpet
stainChemistry: Combination stains contain protein, fat, and tannin or mineral components. Mud contains organic material (plant matter, tannins), clay/mineral particles, and sometimes protein components. Each component requires different chemistry: protein needs detergent, fat needs solvent, tannin needs peroxide, minerals need acid. Standard approach: let mud dry completely first (wet scrubbing drives particles deeper), then dry-brush, then detergent, then solvent, then tannin treatment.
whyThisWorks: This combination stain contains multiple components — organic pigments, clay, and tannins — that each need separate treatment. Mud soaks through carpet into the padding below. Surface cleaning won't reach it — the stain hides underneath and wicks back up as the material dries. On wool carpet the chemistry has to stay cold and gentle: wool keratin sets permanently with hot water and is destroyed by chlorine bleach. Dwell time matters more than scrubbing force.
defaultAssumption: Treat as unknown age / possibly dried.

steps: 1. Let the mud dry completely. Once dry, brush and vacuum the loose dry material out of the wool pile before any wet step.

2. Apply NSD and work gently into the stain. NSD is wool-safe at neutral pH — it targets the protein component and emulsifies surface oils. Blot thoroughly between passes. Dwell 5 minutes. Use cold water only.
3. Apply POG or VDS to extract the oil and grease component. Blot with clean absorbent cloth.
4. If browning or color remains, apply Tannin to target the plant-based pigments. Tamp gently — do not scrub the wool pile.
5. Apply diluted acetic acid (1:10) to neutralize residual chemistry and restore the fiber. Blot dry.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold only — hot water sets the protein component permanently and felts wool fibers
warnings: Never use chlorine bleach on wool — destroys keratin permanently
Never use hot or warm water — felts the wool and sets the stain
Never use protease enzyme cleaners on wool — they digest keratin like they digest the stain
Skip the dry-brush step and you drive clay particles deeper into the wool pile
Over-agitating the stain — spreads each component deeper and crushes the pile

steps: 1. Let the mud dry completely before touching it. Wet scrubbing drives clay particles deeper into the wool pile.
2. Once fully dry, brush off the loose dry material with a soft brush, then vacuum the area thoroughly.
3. Mix a small amount of wool-safe detergent (a mild, pH-neutral wash like Woolite) into cold water (never hot — hot water felts wool and sets stains).
4. Blot the cold solution onto the stain with a clean white cloth. Do not scrub or rub the wool pile.
5. Let it dwell 5–10 minutes, then blot with a cloth dampened with plain cold water to rinse.
6. Place a stack of paper towels over the spot and weigh them down so the area dries slowly without wicking.
products: OxiClean Versatile Stain Remover (cold water only)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: If the stain is still visible after multiple cold treatments, stop. Wool carpet damage from over-treatment is worse than the original stain. Call a professional carpet cleaner experienced with wool.
warnings: Always blot — never rub or scrub. Rubbing damages wool fibers and crushes the pile.
Cold water only — hot water felts wool and sets the stain permanently.
Never use chlorine bleach on wool carpet — it destroys the fiber.
Let mud dry first and brush it out before any wet step — wet scrubbing drives clay deeper.

neverDo: Use chlorine bleach on wool — destroys keratin permanently
Use hot or warm water — felts wool and sets the protein component
Use protease enzyme cleaners on wool — they digest the fiber
Scrub wet mud — drives clay particles deeper into the wool pile
Apply all treatments simultaneously — creates a harder-to-remove chemical slurry
fiberSensitivities: Wool is a protein fiber. It needs cold water, neutral-pH chemistry, gentle agitation, and zero chlorine bleach. Heat, alkalinity, and chlorine all destroy the fiber permanently.

escalation.when: Sequential cold treating fails, or stain components have cross-linked together, or the wool pile is starting to crush or felt
escalation.whatToTell: Mud stain on wool carpet, combination of organic pigment, clay, and tannins. Treated at home with cold-water NSD only. Please use multi-step sequential protocol with wool-safe chemistry — no chlorine, no heat.
escalation.specialistType: professional carpet cleaner experienced with wool

contextQuestions: Was the mud tracked in from outdoors (clay-heavy) or from a garden/potting soil?
Is the stain fresh or has it dried?
Has anyone already applied hot water, bleach, or an enzyme cleaner to it?
Was there grass mixed with the mud?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Clay-heavy mud is more tenacious on wool and needs the dry-brush step done thoroughly before any wet step. Garden/potting soil often has higher organic content. Grass mixed with mud adds a tannin/chlorophyll component. Wool requires gentler chemistry and zero heat throughout.
